name: Graphical User Interface
description: Composable, reactive GUI components that bind directly to your data mesh

content.title: Graphical User Interface
content.content:
# Graphical User Interface

The secret to maintainable user interfaces is not elaborate frameworks - it's **composable, reactive controls that bind directly to your data mesh**. Traditional UI development struggles with tight coupling between presentation and logic, manual state synchronization, and brittle event handlers that break when data flows change. Teams spend more time fighting framework complexity than solving business problems.

MeshWeaver's GUI architecture takes a different approach: **declarative controls** that describe what the interface should look like, **reactive streams** that automatically propagate changes, and **JsonPointer binding** that eliminates manual synchronization. Build once, compose everywhere. Change your data model, and the UI updates automatically. No event handlers to maintain, no state machines to debug, no DOM manipulation to get wrong.

## Architecture: Control/Skin/View Pattern

MeshWeaver separates interface concerns into three distinct layers that work together:

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Control - Data Model] --> B[Skin - Styling]
    A --> C[View - Rendering]
    B --> C
    C --> D[Reactive Stream]
    D --> E[JsonPointer Binding]
    E --> F[Data Mesh]
    F --> D
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#ffe1f5
    style D fill:#e1ffe1
    style E fill:#f5e1ff
    style F fill:#ffe1e1
```

**Controls** are immutable record types that define structure - what elements exist and how they relate. **Skins** provide styling and appearance - colors, spacing, themes. **Views** are Razor components that render controls with skins. When data changes in the mesh, reactive streams notify views, which automatically re-render with updated controls.

## Core GUI Components

| Component | Purpose | Key Feature |
|-----------|---------|-------------|
| **Controls** | Declarative UI models | Immutable records with fluent builders |
| **Views** | Blazor rendering components | Automatic updates via reactive streams |
| **Skins** | Customizable styling | Theme-aware appearance configuration |
| **JsonPointer Binding** | Path-based data binding | Two-way synchronization with data mesh |
| **Reactive Streams** | Change propagation | `IObservable<T>` for automatic UI updates |

## Example 1: EditFormView - Forms with Validation

Consider a claims adjuster updating claim details. Traditional approaches require writing validation logic, managing form state, handling submission, and synchronizing with backend - dozens of lines of boilerplate.

With MeshWeaver:

```csharp
var editForm = Controls.EditForm("/claims/12345")
    .WithField(Controls.TextField()
        .WithLabel("Claim Amount")
        .WithValue(new JsonPointerReference("/amount"))
        .WithRequired(true))
    .WithField(Controls.TextField()
        .WithLabel("Description")
        .WithValue(new JsonPointerReference("/description"))
        .WithMultiline(true))
    .WithSubmitButton("Save Claim");
```

**What happens**: EditFormView renders a Fluent UI form with automatic validation. When the adjuster types, changes flow to the data mesh via JsonPointer. On submit, the stream validates and persists. If validation fails, error messages appear automatically. If it succeeds, a toast notification confirms. The entire synchronization cycle - form to data mesh to backend - happens declaratively.

**Benefits**: No event handlers. No state management. No manual validation. Built-in accessibility. Professional appearance with zero styling code.

## Example 2: DataGridView - Large Dataset Display

Analyzing thousands of claims requires performance and flexibility. Traditional grids need careful optimization, custom column rendering, pagination logic, and sorting handlers.

With MeshWeaver:

```csharp
var grid = Controls.DataGrid(claimsArray)
    .WithColumn(new PropertyColumnControl<string>()
        .WithProperty("ClaimId")
        .WithTitle("ID")
        .WithSortable(true))
    .WithColumn(new PropertyColumnControl<decimal>()
        .WithProperty("Amount")
        .WithTitle("Amount")
        .WithFormat("C"))
    .WithColumn(new TemplateColumnControl<ClaimStatus>()
        .WithTitle("Status")
        .WithCellTemplate(claim => 
            Controls.Badge(claim.Status.ToString())
                .WithAppearance(claim.Status == "Approved" 
                    ? Appearance.Success 
                    : Appearance.Warning)))
    .WithVirtualize(true)
    .WithPagination(true);
```

**What happens**: DataGridView renders a high-performance Fluent DataGrid with virtual scrolling - only visible rows exist in DOM. Sorting, pagination, and column resizing work automatically. Custom templates (like the status badge) compose other controls. When underlying data streams update, the grid refreshes affected rows without full re-render.

**Benefits**: Virtual scrolling handles 100,000+ rows smoothly. Responsive column sizing. Built-in sorting and filtering. Template columns enable any UI complexity. Performance optimizations handled by the framework.

## Example 3: CodeEditorView - Advanced Integration

Document processing workflows often need schema editing - JSON, YAML, or code snippets. Integrating Monaco editor traditionally requires managing editor lifecycle, preventing update loops, and synchronizing edits back to state.

With MeshWeaver:

```csharp
var editor = Controls.CodeEditor()
    .WithLanguage("json")
    .WithValue(new JsonPointerReference("/documentSchema"))
    .WithHeight("400px")
    .WithPlaceholder("Enter JSON schema...");
```

**What happens**: CodeEditorView renders Monaco editor with syntax highlighting for JSON. JsonPointer binds to `/documentSchema` in the data mesh. When the user types, changes debounce and update the mesh. When external code updates the schema (perhaps an AI agent), the editor content refreshes - but only if different, preventing cursor disruption. The `isUpdatingFromStream` flag prevents update loops.

**Benefits**: Professional editing experience (IntelliSense, bracket matching, linting). Two-way binding with loop prevention. Language support for dozens of formats. Minimal integration code.

## Reactive Data Flow

All three examples share the same reactive pattern:

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant View
    participant Control
    participant Stream
    participant DataMesh
    
    User->>View: Interaction (type, click, select)
    View->>Control: Update control state
    Control->>Stream: UpdatePointer(value, JsonPointerReference)
    Stream->>DataMesh: Apply change
    DataMesh->>Stream: Notify subscribers
    Stream->>View: OnNext(updated data)
    View->>View: Re-render with new control
    View->>User: Updated interface
```

The user never sees this complexity. They interact with a form, grid, or editor. Behind the scenes, controls update streams, streams update the data mesh, the mesh notifies all subscribed views, and views re-render. If multiple views show the same data (perhaps a grid and a detail form), both update automatically when either changes.

## Fluent Builders: Readable Configuration

Controls use **fluent builder pattern** for maximum readability:

```csharp
var claimDialog = Controls.Dialog(
        Controls.EditForm("/claims/new")
            .WithField(Controls.TextField()
                .WithLabel("Policyholder Name")
                .WithValue(new JsonPointerReference("/policyholderName"))
                .WithRequired(true))
            .WithField(Controls.DatePicker()
                .WithLabel("Incident Date")
                .WithValue(new JsonPointerReference("/incidentDate")))
            .WithSubmitButton("Create Claim"),
        "New Claim"
    )
    .WithSize("M")
    .WithClosable(true)
    .WithDismissible(false);

host.UpdateArea(DialogControl.DialogArea, claimDialog);
```

Every method returns a new immutable control with the change applied. Configuration reads like a description of the interface. No setters, no mutable state, no order dependencies. Compose dialogs containing forms containing fields - arbitrary nesting depth.

## Traditional vs MeshWeaver GUI

| Aspect | Traditional UI | MeshWeaver GUI |
|--------|---------------|----------------|
| **Data Binding** | Manual event handlers, onChange callbacks, state setters | Automatic JsonPointer binding with reactive streams |
| **State Management** | Complex state machines, Redux, local component state | Reactive streams & dimensions - state lives in data mesh |
| **Component Reuse** | Copy-paste or deep inheritance hierarchies | Declarative controls with fluent composition |
| **Updates** | Manual DOM manipulation, forceUpdate(), key tricks | Automatic reactive updates via IObservable subscriptions |
| **Separation of Concerns** | Mixed presentation logic and business logic | Clean Control/Skin/View separation by design |

## Best Practices

**Declarative over Imperative**: Describe what the interface should be, not how to build it. `Controls.Button("Save")` instead of `var button = new Button(); button.Text = "Save"; button.Click += ...`

**Composition over Inheritance**: Build complex UIs by composing simple controls. Dialogs contain forms. Forms contain fields. Fields contain labels and inputs. Arbitrary depth, no inheritance complexity.

**Reactive Binding Eliminates Sync**: Don't write synchronization code. JsonPointer binding and reactive streams handle it. Form updates mesh. Mesh updates all subscribed views. Circular updates prevented automatically.

**Type Safety**: Controls are strongly typed records. Compile-time errors catch configuration mistakes. `WithValue()` takes `JsonPointerReference`, not strings.

**Fluent APIs for Readability**: Configuration should read like documentation. `Controls.DataGrid(data).WithVirtualize(true).WithPagination(true)` is self-explanatory.

**Cascading Parameters for Context**: Don't pass data through every component. Use cascading `DataContext`, `Model`, `ThemeMode`. Child views inherit context automatically.

## The Result: Less Code, Fewer Bugs

MeshWeaver's GUI architecture turns UI development from an integration challenge into a composition exercise. Build controls declaratively. Bind to data mesh with JsonPointers. Let reactive streams handle synchronization. The framework manages complexity - lifecycle, updates, subscriptions, cleanup - while you focus on business value.

When requirements change (and they always do), you change control definitions. Data model evolves? Update JsonPointer paths. New validation rules? Add them to streams. The UI stays synchronized automatically. No state management bugs. No forgotten event handlers. No manual DOM updates that miss edge cases.

Complex, data-driven interfaces that once took weeks now take days. And they stay maintainable as complexity grows, because the architecture scales with composability rather than fighting it.
